title: Apply: Presidency
description: Apply your knowledge about how the presidency works in this set of multiple-choice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Arthur Schlesinger’s *The Imperial Presidency* (1973).**

The [[☃ definition 1]] of inside information—"if you only knew what we know"—was a most effective way to defend the national-security [[☃ definition 2]] and prevent democratic control of foreign policy . . . 

The power [of the government] to leak [information] meant the power to tell the people what it served the government’s purpose that they should know . . . 

The power to withhold and the power to leak led on [[☃ definition 3]] to the power to lie. The secrecy system instilled in the executive branch the idea that foreign policy was no one's business [[☃ definition 4]] its own, and the uncontrolled secrecy made it easy for lying to become routine.

**Based on the text, which statement would the author most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the quality of being interesting and mysterious
control by only one person or group
inevitably; in a way that can't be stopped

choices.content: The executive branch has too much power in implementing foreign policy.
The executive branch should not be at all involved in foreign policy.
Secrecy is necessary for effective government action abroad.
Government leaks ensure that corruption in the executive branch is exposed.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Arthur Schlesinger’s *The Imperial Presidency* (1973).**

The [[☃ definition 1]] of inside information—"if you only knew what we know"—was a most effective way to defend the national-security [[☃ definition 2]] and prevent democratic control of foreign policy . . . 

The power [of the government] to leak [information] meant the power to tell the people what it served the government’s purpose that they should know . . . 

The power to withhold and the power to leak led on [[☃ definition 3]] to the power to lie. The secrecy system instilled in the executive branch the idea that foreign policy was no one's business [[☃ definition 4]] its own, and the uncontrolled secrecy made it easy for lying to become routine.

**Which governmental policy best addresses the problem identified in the excerpt?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the quality of being interesting and mysterious
control by only one person or group
inevitably; in a way that can't be stopped

choices.content: The Gulf of Tonkin Resolution of 1964, which authorized the president to do whatever was necessary to aid U.S. allies in Southeast Asia
The War Powers Resolution of 1973, which aimed to give Congress more say in whether U.S. troops were deployed abroad
The Obama administration’s 2016 decision not to enforce immigration laws mandating the deportation of undocumented young people
The Patriot Act of 2001, which expanded government surveillance measures in order to combat terrorism

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Twelfth Amendment of the U.S. Constitution.**

The Electors shall meet in their [[☃ definition 1]] states and vote by ballot for President and Vice-President, one of whom, at least, shall not be an inhabitant of the same state with themselves; they shall name in their ballots the person voted for as President, and in distinct ballots the person voted for as Vice-President, and they shall make distinct lists of all persons voted for as President, and of all persons voted for as Vice-President, and of the number of votes for each, which lists they shall sign and certify, and transmit sealed to the seat of the government of the United States, directed to the President of the Senate. . .

**Which statement best summarizes the change reflected in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The electors could vote by secret ballot, rather than voting publicly.
The electors would not have to align their votes with those of the voters from their state.
The electors would vote for the president and vice president separately.
The electors could not vote for any candidates from their home state.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Twelfth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution.**

. . . if no person [has a] majority [in the Electoral College], then from the persons having the highest numbers not exceeding three on the list of those voted for as President, the House of Representatives shall choose immediately, by ballot, the President. But in choosing the President, the votes shall be taken by states, the representation from each state having one vote . . .

**According to this amendment, how is the president chosen if no candidate receives a majority of votes by the Electoral College?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A joint body of senators and representatives will choose among the top three candidates that advance from the Electoral College vote.
The Electors will meet again and cast new ballots for president and vice president, with the House of Representatives overseeing this new vote.
Every representative in the House gets one vote to determine which of the top three candidates should become president.
The House of Representatives votes on the top three vote-getters, with each state having one vote.

assessment_items.question_content:
Presidential candidate |  Electoral Vote | Popular Vote 
- | - | - | - 
**Democratic Party Candidate** |  48.2% | 48.5%
**Republican Party Candidate** |  51.8% | 46.9% 
**Green Party Candidate ** |  0% | 3.2%
**Reform Party Candidate** | 0% | 1.4%

**Based on the results shown in the table, who won the election?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Democratic Party candidate
The Republican Party candidate
The Green Party candidate
The Reform Party candidate

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 69 by Alexander Hamilton. It was published in 1788.**

The president is to be commander in chief of the army and navy of the United States. In this respect his authority would be [[☃ definition 1]] the same with that of the king of Great Britain, but in substance much inferior to it. It would amount to nothing more than the supreme command and direction of the military and naval forces, as first general and admiral of the confederacy; while that of the British king extends to the *declaring* of war, and to the *raising* and *regulating* of fleets and armies; all which by the constitution under consideration, would [[☃ definition 2]] to the legislature. 

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The president’s military powers are identical to those of the British king, but any actions must be approved by Congress.
The president has little authority to command the military during peacetime but has more power during wartime.
The president oversees the military but does not have the same powers as the British king, as those powers are given to the legislature.
The president has approval power over any military action that Congress takes, as the president is commander in chief of the military.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 69 by Alexander Hamilton. It was published in 1788.**

The president is to be commander in chief of the army and navy of the United States. In this respect his authority would be [[☃ definition 1]] the same with that of the king of Great Britain, but in substance much inferior to it. It would amount to nothing more than the supreme command and direction of the military and naval forces, as first general and admiral of the confederacy; while that of the British king extends to the *declaring* of war, and to the *raising* and *regulating* of fleets and armies; all which by the constitution under consideration, would [[☃ definition 2]] to the legislature. 

**Based on the excerpt, which statement best describes the distribution of military powers under the U.S. Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The legislature has the power to declare war and fund the military, while the president leads the military.
The legislature has full control over the military, but the president can override any vetoes on military spending.
The president is responsible for funding the military, while the legislature directs military action and declares war.
The president has full control over all aspects of the military, while the legislature has no military powers.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer* (1952), written by Justice Hugo Black.**

[T]he order cannot properly be sustained as an exercise of the President’s military power as Commander in Chief of the Armed Forces. The Government attempts to do so by citing a number of cases upholding broad powers in military commanders engaged in day-to-day fighting in a [[☃ definition 1]]. Such cases need not concern us here. Even though “theater of war” be an expanding concept, we cannot with faithfulness to our constitutional system hold that the Commander in Chief of the Armed Forces has the ultimate power as such to take possession of private property in order to keep labor disputes from stopping production. This is a job for the Nation’s lawmakers, not for its military authorities.

**Which statement best describes the effect of the ruling in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the place where a war is actually occurring

choices.content: The role of commander in chief gives the president broad powers over the production of military equipment.
The president can use their military powers to ensure peace on home soil, including by settling labor disputes.
There are some limits to the president’s military powers but only in times of peace.
There are limits to the president’s powers in their role as commander in chief.

assessment_items.question_content:
** In this excerpt from  Federalist No. 68, Alexander Hamilton discusses the process of choosing the president. He writes about the advantage of the electoral process using the Electoral College rather than a direct election. **

It was desirable, that the sense of the people should operate in the choice of the person to whom so important a trust was to be confided. This end will be [[☃ definition 1]] by committing the right of making it, not to any pre-established body, but to men, chosen by the people for the special purpose. . . .

It was equally desirable, that the [[☃ definition 2]] election should be made by men most capable of analyzing the qualities adapted to the [[☃ definition 3]], and acting under circumstances favorable to deliberation and to a [[☃ definition 4]] combination of all the reasons and inducements, which were proper to govern their choice. A small number of persons, selected by their fellow citizens from the general mass, will be most likely to possess the information and [[☃ definition 5]] [[☃ definition 6]] such complicated investigations.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: position; the job someone needs to do

choices.content: The process for choosing the president ensures that voters are not involved, as they don’t have the necessary information and qualifications.
The process for choosing the president ensures that the people have a say, but the knowledgeable electors make the final choice.
The election process ensures that the most popular and well-known candidates are eligible for the presidency.
The election process ensures that large numbers of voters participate and that the candidate chosen represents the will of the people.

assessment_items.question_content:
** In this excerpt from  Federalist No. 68, Alexander Hamilton discusses the process of choosing the president. He writes about the advantage of the electoral process using the Electoral College rather than a direct election. **

It was desirable, that the sense of the people should operate in the choice of the person to whom so important a trust was to be confided. This end will be [[☃ definition 1]] by committing the right of making it, not to any pre-established body, but to men, chosen by the people for the special purpose. . . .

It was equally desirable, that the [[☃ definition 2]] election should be made by men most capable of analyzing the qualities adapted to the [[☃ definition 3]], and acting under circumstances favorable to deliberation and to a [[☃ definition 4]] combination of all the reasons and inducements, which were proper to govern their choice. A small number of persons, selected by their fellow citizens from the general mass, will be most likely to possess the information and [[☃ definition 5]] [[☃ definition 6]] such complicated investigations.

**According to the excerpt, what was Hamilton’s vision for the Electoral College?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: position; the job someone needs to do

choices.content: To ensure that Congress was in charge of the presidential election
To guarantee that the winner of the popular vote became president
To eliminate any vote by the people, as voters would not have the necessary expertise
To balance the will of the people with the expertise of those best qualified to select the president

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Luther Martin’s essay published in the *Maryland Gazette* in 1788.**

[T]hose who wished as far as possible to establish a *national* instead of a *federal* government, made repeated attempts to have the president chosen by the people at large; on this the [[☃ definition 1]] of the convention was taken, I think not less than three times while I was there, and as often rejected, but within the last [[☃ definition 2]] of their session, they obtained the alteration in the matter it now stands, by which the large States have a very [[☃ definition 3]] influence in the appointment of the president. 

—There is no case where the States will have an equal voice in the appointment of the president, except where two [candidates] shall have an equal number of votes, and those a majority of the whole number of electors, a case very unlikely to happen, or where no [candidate] has a majority of the votes; in these instances the house of representatives are to choose by ballot, each State having an equal voice, but they are confined in the last instance to the five [candidates] who have the greatest number of votes, which gives the largest States [[☃ definition 4]] of having the president chose under their nomination.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Electoral College system gives large states an advantage over smaller states when it comes to selecting the president.
The use of a popular vote to determine the president will lead to larger states having too much influence over who becomes president.
The Electoral College system is fair because it gives each state an equal number of electoral votes to cast in a presidential election.
The use of a popular vote is the best way to determine the president, as it ensures the president is chosen by the people, not legislators.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Luther Martin’s essay published in the *Maryland Gazette* in 1788.**

[T]hose who wished as far as possible to establish a *national* instead of a *federal* government, made repeated attempts to have the president chosen by the people at large; on this the [[☃ definition 1]] of the convention was taken, I think not less than three times while I was there, and as often rejected, but within the last [[☃ definition 2]] of their session, they obtained the alteration in the matter it now stands, by which the large States have a very [[☃ definition 3]] influence in the appointment of the president. 

—There is no case where the States will have an equal voice in the appointment of the president, except where two [candidates] shall have an equal number of votes, and those a majority of the whole number of electors, a case very unlikely to happen, or where no [candidate] has a majority of the votes; in these instances the house of representatives are to choose by ballot, each State having an equal voice, but they are confined in the last instance to the five [candidates] who have the greatest number of votes, which gives the largest States [[☃ definition 4]] of having the president chose under their nomination.

**Which fact about the Electoral College best supports Martin’s argument that “large States will have a very undue influence in the appointment of the president”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The number of electors each state receives is determined by the House of Representatives before each election.
The number of electors each state receives is based on the physical size of the state.
The number of electors is determined by the number of registered voters in each state.
The number of electors is based on the number of representatives a state has in Congress.
